narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your undergraduate studies and law school aspirations, including choosing full-time enrollment and earning a respectable undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical student's 3.21 average. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 72% chance that you would fail the bar exam, leading to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the lowest 10th percentile—far below the middle-of-the-pack averages around the 5th or 6th decile for most applicants—which stood out as the strongest signals pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your LSAT score of 27, notably lower than the dataset's average of about 37, also contributed in that direction, reinforcing concerns about exam readiness. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA pulled the prediction somewhat toward success, as did your family income bracket, suggesting these provided a helpful counterbalance compared to typical students. Your full-time status had a minor favorable influence, aligning with the vast majority of applicants who share that path. Overall, while your GPA and background held you closer to the pass threshold than many, the low decile rankings and LSAT score were the dominant factors tipping the scales against you when stacked against historical patterns. It's tough to hear, especially when you've worked hard, but this model helps schools avoid mismatched placements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications, and gathering letters highlighting your GPA resilience. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting schools with holistic reviews or bridging programs—keep pursuing your goals, as your story isn't defined by one prediction. We're rooting for you.
